tool.worst_line: The cap subsidizes exactly this week.
tool.why: Sales math dressed up as urgency, delivered with lawyerly certainty.

body_snatcher.worst_line: There is no arrangement of the remaining weeks that reconstructs it.
body_snatcher.why: Faceless, overpolished copy; Matti sounds replaced by a management consultant.

familiarity.why: Enrollment explains today, but the broadcast monologue and banner CTA kill the friend premise.

verdict.one_fix: Alex — you said the Maratona is the one. Your training window starts now. Want me to tell you honestly whether you need a plan? Reply with your weekly hours.

tool.worst_line: At $15 per week of training, the short runway is also the inexpensive one.
tool.why: A polished price justification followed by a shouty CTA is unmistakably sales copy.

body_snatcher.why: Faceless consultant language, broken placeholders, and dense feature copy sound nothing like Matti.

familiarity.worst_line: Alex — the Maratona is approximately weeks out.
familiarity.why: Enrollment explains today, but the email never acknowledges what Alex did or asks anything personal.

verdict.one_fix: Rewrite around the enrollment trigger: “Alex — you’re looking at Maratona with {weeks_out} weeks left. What’s your biggest problem: fitness, pacing, or fueling?”

subject: three variables that decide your fondo

tool.worst_line: Every race profile covers course, pacing, and prep for that specific event: find yours .
tool.why: The abrupt generic CTA reveals the lesson as a sales setup.

body_snatcher.worst_line: They convert existing fitness into availability.
body_snatcher.why: Polished consultant language; specific information, but no recognizable human speaking.

familiarity.worst_line: P.S. Next: the race-week don't list.
familiarity.why: A scheduled nurture installment teasing another installment, with no personal reason to arrive today.

verdict.one_fix: Alex — which fondo are you riding? Send me the name and I’ll tell you where people usually cook themselves.

tool.worst_line: Race week adds nothing. It can only subtract.
tool.why: Punchy, but the absolute certainty sounds performed rather than candid.

body_snatcher.why: Useful but faceless; any competent cycling coach could have written this polished checklist.

familiarity.worst_line: Your race's profile marks where patience pays: the database .
familiarity.why: Nothing proves Alex races soon; the broken generic CTA exposes a scheduled nurture blast.

verdict.one_fix: Anchor it to Alex's actual race and one course-specific pacing trap: “Your race is Saturday. That first climb is bait—cap it at X.”

subject: software models the rider, not the race

tool.why: The shouty CTA breaks an otherwise restrained pitch and sounds like conversion copy.

body_snatcher.why: The polished feature stack and objection script sound like a competent marketing department, not Matti texting Alex.

familiarity.worst_line: This is the sales email. One short follow-up next week.
familiarity.why: It explicitly exists because the sequence calendar says day eight, not because something happened for Alex today.

verdict.one_fix: Replace the scheduled sales brochure with a race-specific note: “Alex — what race did you circle? Send it over. I’ll tell you whether your runway looks sane.”

tool.worst_line: If the answer is “eventually”: eventually is the expensive version of yes.
tool.why: Manufactured urgency dressed as arithmetic; it corners the reader instead of helping them decide.

body_snatcher.why: Distinctly Roadie Labs, but so polished and declarative it barely sounds like Matti texting.

familiarity.worst_line: The promised follow-up. After this, the subject is closed.
familiarity.why: Its real reason for arriving today is sequence timing and another sales attempt.

verdict.one_fix: Cut the closing argument. Ask for Alex’s race date, do the actual calendar math, then say plainly whether waiting matters.

tool.worst_line: That service is what you paid for; the workouts alone were never the scarce part.
tool.why: Sounds like polished value justification after the sale.

body_snatcher.why: Corporate status language makes Matti sound like an order-management system.

familiarity.why: Alex just bought a plan; confirming receipt and setting expectations is timely.

verdict.one_fix: Alex — I’ve got your questionnaire. I’m building around your race, FTP, and actual available hours. You’ll have it within 48 hours.

tool.worst_line: The plan says rest, you rest.
tool.why: Commands Alex like a subordinate; the certainty feels coach-performative, not friendly.

body_snatcher.worst_line: This is the instruction most often ignored and most expensive to ignore.
body_snatcher.why: Faceless coaching copy with no Matti-specific mistake, hedge, deadpan aside, or methodological detail.

familiarity.worst_line: Proper check-in at the end of week 2.
familiarity.why: Week-one calibration is timely, but this scheduled announcement never asks how Alex's actual rides feel.

verdict.one_fix: Alex — quick note because week one is where I usually turn a measurement into an ego test: hit the prescribed watts exactly. How have Zone 2 efforts felt so far?

tool.why: Direct, useful, and unsalesy; the bluntness earns its place.

body_snatcher.worst_line: Two weeks of data exist now. This email collects it.
body_snatcher.why: The robotic opener briefly sounds like automated account management, not Matti.

familiarity.why: Two weeks of training creates a specific, useful reason to check calibration now.

verdict.one_fix: Alex — you’ve got two weeks on the plan now, so I want to check where I guessed wrong. Three quick ones:

tool.worst_line: The data on this is not ambiguous.
tool.why: Unqualified certainty sounds like a coach performing authority, not a friend showing the evidence.

body_snatcher.worst_line: It is also where the fitness is actually manufactured.
body_snatcher.why: Distinctive deadpan appears, but much of the copy remains polished coaching-broadcast language.

familiarity.why: Week three creates a real reason to discuss stalled motivation, missed targets, and the approaching recovery week.

verdict.one_fix: Replace the authority flex with evidence and humility: “I don’t know your exact fatigue curve yet, but skipping recovery usually shows up as flat workouts, not bonus fitness.”

tool.why: Sounds like a survey administrator implying other answers are dishonest.

body_snatcher.worst_line: Six weeks of plan data on your side; this email collects one number for ours.
body_snatcher.why: Brand-specific scoring helps, but the polished NPS script barely sounds like Matti.

familiarity.worst_line: 0 to 10 — how likely are you to recommend the plan to another rider?
familiarity.why: This exists because day 42 triggered an NPS survey, not because Matti has something personal to say.

verdict.one_fix: Alex — six weeks in, did the plan actually help? Reply 0–10. If it missed, tell me where; that’s more useful.

tool.why: Direct, useful, and unsentimental. It asks for nothing except enough context to help.

body_snatcher.why: Specific Roadie Labs substance, but this phrase sounds like polished institutional copy, not Matti texting.

familiarity.why: Alex just requested matches; helping interpret them before committing is a credible reason to write today.

verdict.one_fix: Replace “Ties are worth adjudicating” with: “If two are tied, send me both names. I’ll tell you what the quiz flattened.”

tool.worst_line: That is how the database stays worth reading.
tool.why: Self-important closing turns a useful correction request into a brand credibility pitch.

body_snatcher.worst_line: Same distance category, opposite fitness demands.
body_snatcher.why: Polished expert copy with no hesitation, personal stake, or recognizable Matti texture.

familiarity.worst_line: Read the course section of the profile twice.
familiarity.why: Nothing happened today; this is a scheduled lesson disguised as personal advice.

verdict.one_fix: Give today a real trigger: “Alex — I re-read the Maratona profile and one section could screw up your pacing: the course demands.”

subject: software models the rider, not the race

tool.why: The feature stack, price, and CTA turn bluntness into a polished sales pitch.

body_snatcher.worst_line: The output is fitness in a generic shape. Races are not generic.
body_snatcher.why: Specific and credible, but polished positioning language overwhelms Matti's human voice.

familiarity.worst_line: This is the sales email. One short follow-up next week. Then the subject is closed.
familiarity.why: It explicitly exists because the sequence calendar says to sell today.

verdict.one_fix: Replace the entire pitch with: “Alex — the Maratona has 4,000-ish meters of climbing, so generic ‘hilly ride’ training is pretty useless. Send me your FTP and honest weekly hours. I’ll tell you whether your runway looks workable before I try to sell you anything.”

tool.worst_line: What to expect from this list: mostly signal.
tool.why: Self-congratulation plus list-management language makes the confidence feel marketed.

body_snatcher.worst_line: The free things worth your time, in order:
body_snatcher.why: The numbered resource tour is polished onboarding copy, not Matti talking to Alex.

familiarity.worst_line: Two emails about the training plans we sell — one next week, one short follow-up after it.
familiarity.why: Enrollment justifies today, but announcing the campaign schedule exposes the broadcast machinery.

verdict.one_fix: Alex — you just joined, so start here: [database]. What race are you considering? Send me the name and I’ll tell you what your remaining weeks can realistically do.

subject: how many carbs does a gran fondo actually take?

tool.why: Clinical and blunt, with no hype, groveling, or fake excitement.

body_snatcher.worst_line: Alex — A question with a numeric answer: how many gels does a 7-hour gran fondo actually require?
body_snatcher.why: The deadpan data voice fits, but the polished lecture opener sounds authored for a list.

familiarity.worst_line: P.S. Next: the lowest-rated race in the database, and why publishing that number is the entire point of Roadie Labs.
familiarity.why: A scheduled lesson teasing the next installment has no person-specific reason to arrive today.

verdict.one_fix: Open with Alex’s signup context and a real question: “Alex — you signed up from the {race_name} page. How long do you expect to be out there?”

tool.why: Blunt, restrained, and never begs for attention or inflates the reader.

body_snatcher.worst_line: That is not a weakness of the system; it is the system.
body_snatcher.why: Specific and credible, but polished manifesto language sometimes replaces Matti’s human presence.

familiarity.worst_line: Alex — The lowest score in the Roadie Labs database is 44 out of 100: L'Étape Indonesia.
familiarity.why: Nothing connects this day-five lecture to Alex’s signup, race, question, or decision today.

verdict.one_fix: Rewrite the opener around Alex’s signup context: “Alex — before you use our ratings to book a flight, here’s the race we’d tell you not to fly for.”

subject: software models the rider, not the race

tool.why: The blunt honesty helps, but the capitalized CTA and exhaustive pitch still feel salesy.

body_snatcher.why: Distinctive evidence and concessions survive, but sales-page structure overwhelms Matti's conversational identity.

familiarity.worst_line: This is the sales email. One short follow-up next week.
familiarity.why: Its reason for arriving is sequence timing, openly admitted, not anything Alex did today.

verdict.one_fix: Alex — you signed up from the [race] page. Still racing it? Reply with your weekly hours and I’ll tell you whether a plan is justified.

tool.worst_line: If the answer is “eventually”: eventually is the expensive version of yes.
tool.why: A sharp sales closer disguised as financial advice; it pressures instead of helping.

body_snatcher.why: Specific math, methodological candor, and deadpan verdicts sound distinctly Roadie Labs.

familiarity.worst_line: Alex — The promised follow-up. After this, the subject is closed.
familiarity.why: Day-ten repitch language exposes the sequence machinery; no personal reason makes today matter.

verdict.one_fix: Alex — send me your race date and I’ll tell you honestly whether starting now buys anything useful.
